prompt: Find all integers $k \ge 2$ for which there exists $k$-element set $M$ of positive integers such that the product of all numbers in $M$ is divisible by the sum of any two (different) numbers from $M$.
response_text:
Let
\[
P=\prod_{x\in M}x
\]
and suppose that for every two distinct \(a,b\in M\), we have
\[
a+b\mid P.
\]
We seek all \(k\ge 2\) for which such a \(k\)-element set \(M\) exists.

We will show that this is possible for every \(k\ge 2\).

## Construction

Take
\[
M=\{2,\,2^2,\,2^4,\,2^8,\dots,2^{2^{k-1}}\}.
\]
This set has \(k\) distinct positive integers.

Let two different elements be
\[
a=2^{2^i},\qquad b=2^{2^j}\qquad (i<j).
\]
Then
\[
a+b=2^{2^i}+2^{2^j}=2^{2^i}\bigl(1+2^{2^j-2^i}\bigr).
\]
Since \(j>i\), the number \(2^{2^j-2^i}\) is even, so \(1+2^{2^j-2^i}\) is odd. Hence
\[
v_2(a+b)=2^i.
\]
So each pairwise sum is of the form \(2^{2^i}\cdot(\text{odd})\).

Now consider the product
\[
P=\prod_{t=0}^{k-1}2^{2^t}=2^{\,\sum_{t=0}^{k-1}2^t}=2^{2^k-1}.
\]
This is a pure power of \(2\). Therefore, for \(a+b\) to divide \(P\), its odd part must be \(1\). So the above construction does **not** work directly.

So we need a better idea.

---

## Better construction

Let
\[
M=\{1,2,2^2,2^4,\dots,2^{2^{k-2}}\}.
\]
Again \(|M|=k\). Let
\[
P=\prod_{x\in M}x=2^{1+2+4+\cdots+2^{k-2}}=2^{2^{k-1}-1}.
\]
But now sums like \(1+2^{2^i}\) are odd and greater than \(1\), so they also cannot divide \(P\). Thus this also fails.

So we need a fundamentally different construction, with the product containing many odd divisors.

---

## Key idea

We build \(M\) recursively.

Suppose \(M=\{a_1,\dots,a_k\}\) works, with product \(P\). Let
\[
N=P\cdot\prod_{1\le i<j\le k}(a_i+a_j).
\]
Choose a new element
\[
x=N.
\]
Then for every old element \(a_i\),
\[
a_i+x \mid a_iN+N = N(a_i+1)
\]
is not immediate, so this naive step does not work.

Instead we use a standard Sylvester-type trick: choose numbers so that every pairwise sum is itself one of the factors of the total product.

Take
\[
M=\{d_1,d_2,\dots,d_k\}
\]
where
\[
d_i=\prod_{j\ne i} n_j
\]
for suitable pairwise coprime integers \(n_1,\dots,n_k\).

Then
\[
P=\prod_{i=1}^k d_i=\prod_{i=1}^k n_i^{\,k-1}.
\]
Also
\[
d_i+d_j=\Bigl(\prod_{\ell\ne i,j} n_\ell\Bigr)(n_i+n_j).
\]
So if we choose the \(n_i\) so that \(n_i+n_j\mid n_i^{k-1}n_j^{k-1}\), then \(d_i+d_j\mid P\).

This suggests taking all \(n_i\) powers of \(2\), but then \(n_i+n_j\) has odd part \(>1\). So instead take
\[
n_i=2^{m_i}-1
\]
with carefully chosen \(m_i\), so that \(n_i+n_j\) factors into earlier \(n_t\)'s. A particularly convenient choice is
\[
n_1=1,\quad n_{r+1}=n_1n_2\cdots n_r+1.
\]
Then the \(n_i\) are pairwise coprime and for \(i<j\),
\[
n_j\equiv 1\pmod{n_i},
\]
hence
\[
n_i+n_j\equiv 1\pmod{n_i},
\]
which again does not give divisibility.

So let us abandon this route too.

---

## Simple successful construction

Take
\[
M=\{m,\,2m,\,3m,\dots,km\}
\]
for some positive integer \(m\). Then
\[
P=m^k\cdot k!.
\]
For two distinct elements \(im,jm\),
\[
im+jm=(i+j)m.
\]
Thus we need
\[
(i+j)m\mid m^k k!
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
i+j\mid m^{k-1}k!
\]
for all \(1\le i<j\le k\).

Now every such \(i+j\) lies between \(3\) and \(2k-1\). So it is enough to choose \(m\) such that every integer \(3,4,\dots,2k-1\) divides \(m^{k-1}k!\).

Choose
\[
m=(2k-1)!.
\]
Then certainly every \(i+j\) divides \(m\), hence divides \(m^{k-1}k!\). Therefore
\[
im+jm=(i+j)m \mid m\cdot m^{k-1}k!=m^k k!=P.
\]

So for every \(k\ge 2\), the set
\[
M=\{(2k-1)!,\,2(2k-1)!,\,\dots,\,k(2k-1)!\}
\]
works.

Hence all integers \(k\ge 2\) are possible.

Final answer: \(\boxed{\text{All integers }k\ge 2}\).
